English is the official language throughout, common law governs civil registration, and the Registrar General’s Office issues the death certificate without any translation step. Where death wasn’t from natural causes, the Coroner’s Court has to certify the case before release, standard practice across the Eastern Caribbean.

item.title: Samoa Repatriation: Not to Be Confused with American Samoa
item.description:
Samoa is becoming a more common destination for British travellers, helped by a relatively straightforward connection through Auckland. It’s also one of the few Pacific nations where the consular arrangement isn’t with a British post at all.

Get the country right first

There are two Samoas, and they are entirely different jurisdictions. This page covers the Independent State of Samoa. American Samoa, despite the name, is US territory, and a death there is handled under US consular rules, not British or New Zealand ones. Getting this distinction wrong at the start can send a family down the wrong process entirely.

item.title: San Marino: Europe's Fastest Repatriation Route
item.description:
San Marino claims to be the world’s oldest republic, founded in 301 AD, and at just 61 square kilometres, it’s entirely surrounded by Italy’s Emilia-Romagna and Marche regions. Most British visitors see it as a day trip from the Adriatic coast rather than a standalone destination. When a repatriation is needed, that small size and close relationship with Italy work firmly in a family’s favour.

Why this case moves quickly

Civil registration follows Italian practice closely, and the Ufficio di Stato Civile issues the death certificate in Italian, which needs certified English translation before UK authorities will accept it. That’s the only real documentation friction. For anything other than a natural death, the Corpo della Gendarmeria, San Marino’s police and gendarmerie, has to investigate and clear the case, but this is a well-functioning system that doesn’t tend to introduce long delays.

item.title: Bringing Someone Home from Ivory Coast
item.description:
Abidjan doesn’t look like the rest of the Ivory Coast story you might expect. It’s a large, sophisticated West African city, home to the country’s international airport, several teaching hospitals, and a genuine British diplomatic presence. Most British deaths here happen in or near the city, or in the western tourist zone around Man and the Tai National Park.

What Abidjan gets right

A resident British Embassy means direct consular support is available from the outset, which is more than many West African destinations can offer. Natural deaths register at the local mairie, the town hall. Where something isn’t natural, the case goes to the Institut National de Medecine Legale instead, with post-mortem capacity concentrated at three teaching hospitals in the city.

item.title: Death in Iran: What British-Iranian Families Face
item.description:
Iran is one of the most demanding corridors this service handles, and not for one reason alone. The civil documentation process is unfamiliar to most UK families. Islamic burial custom puts the clock under pressure from the first hour. And underneath both of those sits something most people never anticipate: what British-Iranian dual nationality actually means once someone has died there.

The distinction that changes everything

Iran does not recognise dual nationality. Full stop. If the person who died held both British and Iranian citizenship, Iranian authorities treat them solely as an Iranian national. The British Embassy cannot demand consular access as a right in that scenario, and Iranian officials may decline embassy involvement in documentation or in releasing the remains altogether.

item.title: Honduras Repatriation: The Bay Islands Problem
item.description:
Roatan and Utila built their reputations on diving. The Mesoamerican Barrier Reef runs along this coast, the second largest reef system anywhere, and it draws British divers by the thousand every year. Utila tends to attract younger, budget-conscious visitors working towards a PADI certification. Roatan pulls in a wealthier crowd, cruise passengers included.

That popularity is exactly why most British deaths in Honduras happen here rather than on the mainland. Decompression illness and drowning are the two causes a specialist firm sees most often.

item.title: Kiribati to UK: When Distance Is the Whole Problem
item.description: Some repatriation cases are complicated because of politics or paperwork. Kiribati’s complexity comes almost entirely from geography. This is a nation of 33 atolls and reef islands with a land area of just 811 square kilometres, spread across roughly 3.5 million square kilometres of Pacific Ocean, straddling both the equator and the International Date Line. The capital, South Tarawa, sits in the Gilbert Islands group, and the population, around 119,000, is concentrated there.

item.title: Latvia to UK Repatriation: A More Straightforward Case
item.description: Not every case is a difficult one, and Latvia is a good example of that. It’s a Baltic Sea nation, an EU, NATO, and Schengen member bordering Estonia, Lithuania, Russia, and Belarus, with Riga as its capital and commercial centre. A growing number of British nationals live and work there, and tourism to Riga’s Old Town is well established. For a family facing a death abroad, Latvia is genuinely one of the more manageable places for it to happen.

item.title: Lesotho Repatriation: Why Everything Routes Through Johannesburg
item.description:
Lesotho is one of just three countries anywhere entirely enclosed inside another, sitting wholly within South Africa’s borders. That single fact shapes almost everything about how a repatriation case unfolds here. There’s no direct international air link from Lesotho itself, so everything, without exception, exits through Johannesburg.

The upside is real, though. English is an official language alongside Sesotho, the British High Commission has a resident presence in Maseru, and the administrative path is clear and well established. Most UK cases here involve tourists in the Maluti mountain parks, NGO and development workers, and members of the small professional expatriate community.

item.title: Libya Repatriation: Why the Process Depends on Who Controls the Ground
item.description: Libya sits in a category of its own among the corridors this service handles. There’s no single functioning national government with consistent authority nationwide. There’s no resident British Embassy. Armed groups hold significant territory. And still, British nationals die in Libya, oil workers, journalists, NGO staff, contractors among them, and their families need to bring them home. This page sets out what that process genuinely looks like, without softening it.

item.title: Liechtenstein Repatriation: One of Europe's Fastest
item.description:
Liechtenstein doesn’t have an airport. Not a small one, not a regional strip, none at all. It’s one of only two doubly landlocked countries on earth, the other being Uzbekistan, entirely surrounded by Switzerland and Austria. That single geographic fact makes it, paradoxically, one of the fastest and simplest repatriation cases this service handles.

Getting a body out without an airport

Every repatriation from Liechtenstein starts with a road transfer. Zürich Kloten sits around ninety minutes away and carries multiple daily flights to UK airports, making it the standard routing. Basel-Mulhouse, roughly two hours by road, is the usual alternative. A specialist firm arranges this transfer as part of the overall case. Because the roads are excellent and the distance short, it adds very little time compared with countries where the nearest international airport is hours or days away.

item.title: Lithuania Repatriation: Why Traffic Runs Both Ways
item.description:
Most corridors this service handles run one way: British families bringing someone home from abroad. Lithuania is different. One of the largest Lithuanian communities anywhere in the EU has settled in the UK, so repatriation cases run in both directions, UK to Lithuania as often as the reverse. That’s shaped how well practised the process has become on both ends.

A resident Embassy and organised registration

The British Embassy in Vilnius is resident, reachable on +370 5 246 2900, and provides full consular support without the delay a non-resident arrangement can add. Death registration goes through Registru centras, the Register Centre’s Civil Metrical Records division, which issues the Mirties liudijimas, the Lithuanian death certificate, in Lithuanian. Certified English translation is required before the UK Coroner can use it, and your repatriation firm arranges this as standard.

item.title: Luxembourg Repatriation: A Small, Well-Run Process
item.description:
If someone has died in Luxembourg, there’s real reassurance in one fact straight away: this is one of the fastest, most straightforward repatriations in Europe. Most families bringing someone home from Luxembourg are connected to the EU institutions, the financial sector, or the civil service community concentrated in Kirchberg, Clausen, and Belair, a specific, relatively small population, and the system that supports them works smoothly.

A resident Embassy that knows the system well

The British Embassy in Luxembourg City handles all consular registration directly, on +352 22 98 64. Though small in staff terms, it’s well connected with Luxembourg’s efficient civil authorities, and it registers the death and issues what UK probate needs without the delay a non-resident arrangement often brings.

item.title: Madagascar Repatriation: Why Remote Deaths Take Months
item.description:
A charter flight over the Tsingy de Bemaraha limestone formations, a trek through Ranomafana looking for lemurs, a dive off Nosy Be: this is what draws British visitors to Madagascar. It’s also, unfortunately, where the real complications tend to start when something goes wrong, because most of what makes Madagascar spectacular is also genuinely hard to get out of.

No railway, and roads that disappear

Madagascar has virtually no functioning rail network. It’s roughly the size of France, but most of the country beyond Antananarivo is reachable only by small charter aircraft or by roads that degrade badly, sometimes becoming impassable, during the rainy season from November to April. Every repatriation case outside the capital has to reckon with this before anything else.

item.title: Malawi Repatriation: One Pathologist, One Country
item.description:
Every suspicious or unnatural death in Malawi, and a good many natural deaths that happen in remote locations, has to pass through a single office: the Government Pathologist at Kamuzu Central Hospital in Lilongwe. It’s the only facility in the country with formal forensic pathology capacity, and that single bottleneck shapes almost every timeline this service quotes for Malawi.

A well-defined chain, in practice slower than it looks

On paper, the process is clear. The Registrar General’s Department issues the Malawi Death Certificate. For unnatural or sudden deaths, the Malawi Police Service provides a clearance letter confirming no criminal investigation is outstanding. The Government Pathologist issues the post-mortem report. The Ministry of Health then issues the laissez-passer mortuaire authorising international transport. In practice, capacity constraints at that one pathology office mean the paperwork chain rarely moves as fast as it’s designed to. Backlogs are a documented, recurring feature of the system.

item.title: Mali Repatriation: Why 2023 Changed the Route Home
item.description:
In 2023, Air France suspended its service to Bamako. That single airline decision now shapes every UK-bound repatriation from Mali, forcing every case through a third country instead of a direct European connection, and it’s one of several factors that make Mali one of the longer, more demanding West African corridors this service handles.

Government by transition

Mali has been under military rule since Colonel Assimi Goïta’s coup in May 2021. The Transition government controls every layer of civil administration, the Ministry of Health, civil registrars, and the Police Nationale, and administrative decisions can be delayed without explanation. There is no resident British Embassy; the British Embassy in Dakar, Senegal, provides non-resident consular coverage instead, handling death registration, next-of-kin notification, and liaison with Malian authorities remotely.

item.title: Malta Repatriation: What Actually Adds Time
item.description:
Malta is one of the more manageable places for a British family to face this. Flights to Heathrow or Manchester take three to four hours, the legal process is clear, and the British High Commission in Valletta has handled cases like this many times before. The one thing that genuinely changes a Malta case is whether the duty magistrate orders a post-mortem.

The magistrate’s decision is the hinge point

A Maltese doctor certifies the death first. Any sudden or unnatural death is then referred to the duty magistrate, who decides whether a post-mortem is required before the body can be released. Without one, a case typically completes in eight to twelve days. With one, it can extend to twenty-one days or longer. There’s no way to predict the magistrate’s decision in advance, so it’s worth building both possibilities into your expectations from the outset.

item.title: Marshall Islands: Repatriation Across an Ocean
item.description:
The Republic of the Marshall Islands has a land area of just 181 square kilometres, smaller than the Isle of Wight, spread across an ocean territory of roughly 1.9 million square kilometres. Twenty-nine atolls and five isolated islands, one international gateway. That mismatch between land and ocean is the entire story of a Marshall Islands repatriation.

Everything funnels through Majuro

Amata Kabua International Airport in Majuro, the capital, is the only practical international gateway. A death anywhere else, on one of the outer atolls, means boat or chartered light aircraft transfer to Majuro first. Depending on weather and available transport, that alone can take anywhere from several hours to multiple days. A specialist firm has to establish the exact location of death before giving any time estimate at all.

item.title: Mauritania Repatriation: The 24-Hour Question
item.description:
In Mauritania, almost entirely Sunni Muslim, community expectation strongly favours burial within twenty-four hours of death. That single cultural fact matters more than almost anything else if a family in the UK wants their loved one repatriated rather than buried locally, because the window to communicate that intention through local contacts is genuinely narrow.

Say it early, and say it clearly

If repatriation is the family’s wish, that intent needs to reach local contacts, whoever is present at the death, hospital staff, hotel management, or a tour operator, immediately and unambiguously. Once that’s established, a specialist firm can begin coordinating with Mauritanian authorities before the customary burial window closes.

item.title: Micronesia Repatriation: Four States, One Hopper Flight
item.description:
Chuuk Lagoon is one of the world’s premier wreck diving destinations, a sunken Japanese fleet resting on the seabed since 1944, and it draws a specific, serious kind of British visitor: specialist divers. It also sits in one of four states that make up the Federated States of Micronesia, scattered across 2,600 kilometres of the western Pacific, and that geography defines every repatriation case here before anything else does.

Four states, four separate registration systems

Yap, Chuuk, Pohnpei, and Kosrae, the four FSM states, each run their own civil registration office and death registration system, in English throughout. A death on Yap involves entirely different local authorities to a death in Chuuk Lagoon or on Kosrae, even though all four fall under the same national government. Non-natural deaths bring in the relevant state police for investigation and clearance.

item.title: Moldova Repatriation: The Transnistria Problem
item.description:
Moldova itself is one of the more manageable Eastern European repatriations. It has a resident British Embassy, a functioning civil registration system, and a direct flight to London Luton. Then there’s Transnistria, and everything changes.

Why Transnistria is different

Transnistria sits on Moldova’s eastern border with Ukraine. It has run its own government since 1992, backed by Russia, and no country the UK recognises treats it as anything other than part of Moldova on paper. In practice, Transnistrian authorities issue their own documents and don’t answer to Chisinau. A death there can’t be processed through the normal Moldovan civil registration route. The British Embassy’s reach effectively stops at the administrative line. If this describes your situation, ring the FCDO on 020 7008 5000 straight away rather than trying to work through standard Moldovan paperwork channels; they won’t apply.

item.title: Mongolia Repatriation: When the Body Isn't Near Ulaanbaatar
item.description:
The Gobi desert, the open steppe, and the Altai mountains have made Mongolia a real growth destination for UK adventure travel: trekking, horse riding, overlanding tours. The British Embassy in Ulaanbaatar has handled UK traveller deaths before and knows the process. What it can’t change is the geography.

Recovery comes before anything else

Mongolia is the most sparsely populated country on earth, and outside Ulaanbaatar there are very few paved roads. If someone dies in the Gobi, on the steppe, or in the Altai range, the body has to reach Ulaanbaatar before the standard administrative process can begin. The Mongolian National Emergency Management Agency coordinates the response: helicopter where the location allows it, vehicle convoy where it doesn’t. Expect this stage alone to take one to five days, longer if weather closes operations, which happens fairly often in the Altai and central regions during autumn and spring.

item.title: Mozambique: Why Every Document Gets Translated Twice
item.description:
Mozambique sits in an unusual position for UK families. Commonwealth membership means consular support is there when it’s needed. Portuguese colonial history means the paperwork is a genuine hurdle that most other African corridors don’t have.

Commonwealth support, Portuguese paperwork

The British High Commission in Maputo provides the kind of consular assistance you’d expect from a Commonwealth country. But the death certificate, the post-mortem report, and the export permit all come out in Portuguese, and UK authorities won’t accept any of it without certified English translation. That’s not a formality to skip. Legal and medical Portuguese translators aren’t easy to find quickly from the UK, which is exactly why a specialist firm with established local contacts tends to make the biggest difference here.

item.title: Namibia's Self-Drive Risk and What Comes Next
item.description:
If you’re dealing with a death in Namibia, there’s a fair chance a road accident is part of the story. It’s worth knowing that upfront, because it shapes almost everything about how the next few weeks go.

Why self-drive safaris carry real risk

Namibia draws British visitors for exactly the reason it’s also hard going: it’s vast, remote, and strikingly beautiful in ways that pull people off the main roads. Self-drive tourists commonly cover thousands of kilometres linking Etosha National Park, Sossusvlei, Damaraland, and the Skeleton Coast. Much of that driving happens on gravel roads, far from any town, with no mobile signal. A single-vehicle accident in the Kunene region or along the Skeleton Coast can take hours for emergency services simply to reach.

item.title: Nauru Repatriation: One Flight a Week, via Brisbane
item.description:
Nauru is easy to miss on a map, and that’s part of the point: at twenty-one square kilometres, it’s the smallest island state anywhere, and the whole coastline can be driven in about twenty minutes. It’s also one of the more demanding places to manage a repatriation from, simply because there’s so little infrastructure to work with.

A single airline, a narrow window

Nauru International Airport is served exclusively by Nauru Airlines, flying to Brisbane and to Honiara or Tarawa, and frequency is limited to a handful of services a week. Cargo capacity is restricted too. This shapes the entire case: there’s no alternative carrier to fall back on if timing doesn’t line up, so a firm coordinating a Nauru case has to plan around the schedule that actually exists, not the one that would be convenient.

item.title: Nicaragua: Straightforward Paperwork, Watchful State
item.description:
Nicaragua tends to surprise families in one specific way: the paperwork itself isn’t the hard part. President Daniel Ortega has held power since 2007, and since a violent 2018 crackdown on protests, his government has kept a tight grip on civil society. The FCDO advises against non-essential travel. None of that changes how a death certificate gets issued in Managua. It changes what can happen around it.

The administrative process works

Death registration runs through the Registro del Estado Civil de las Personas, under the Consejo Supremo Electoral, and the Ministerio del Interior issues the export permit once certification and health documentation are complete. In the capital, this is genuinely well-established. It’s a process Nicaraguan officials handle routinely, and it doesn’t grind to a halt just because the wider political situation is tense.

item.title: Niger Since the Coup: What Repatriation Looks Like
item.description:
Niger’s political situation changed sharply in July 2023, when the Presidential Guard removed President Mohamed Bazoum in a coup. The military junta that has governed since, the Conseil National pour la Sauvegarde de la Patrie, controls the country’s institutions directly. For anyone facing a death in Niger now, that reality shapes the whole process.

No resident Embassy, no independent civilian pathway

The British Embassy in Abuja, Nigeria, holds non-resident accreditation for Niger and provides every consular service the case will need. Call the FCDO’s 24-hour line, 020 7008 5000, first. FCDO advises against all but essential travel to the entire country, so travelling out to handle things in person isn’t something to plan around.

item.title: North Korea: What Families Should Realistically Expect
item.description:
This is, without question, one of the rarest and hardest situations a UK family can face. North Korea is the world’s most closed state, foreign nationals can’t move without state permission and assigned minders, and the UK has no embassy there at all. If this is your situation, please call the FCDO straight away on 020 7008 5000. There genuinely isn’t a more useful first step.

Why there’s no UK Embassy to call

The UK closed its options for a resident presence in Pyongyang long ago, and consular support for British nationals now comes through the Swedish Embassy, which handles UK matters by prior arrangement with North Korean authorities. The British Embassy in Seoul, reachable on +82 2 3210 5500, coordinates the wider relationship from outside the country. Neither can operate the way an embassy normally would.

item.title: North Macedonia: Skopje, Ohrid, and the Paper Trail
item.description:
North Macedonia tends to be one of the more manageable Balkan repatriations, and the reasons are fairly specific: a resident British Embassy in Skopje, competent forensic medicine services, and reasonably affordable logistics throughout.

What decides the timeline

A natural death with complete documentation typically clears in ten to fourteen days. Where the Institute of Forensic Medicine, based at Ss. Cyril and Methodius University in Skopje, has to get involved, that stretches to fourteen to twenty-one days. The Institute handles every non-natural death and any natural death where the cause isn’t clear, and it operates to a fair standard for the region. Ministry of Interior clearance has to come through before a funeral director can proceed, usually three to five days for a straightforward accident, longer for a more complex investigation.

item.title: Repatriation from Iraq: Why Region Changes Everything
item.description:
Iraq isn’t one country for repatriation logistics purposes. It’s four or five different environments wearing the same passport stamp, and which one your case falls into decides almost everything about how it unfolds.

The regional picture, honestly stated

The Kurdistan Region, Erbil, Sulaymaniyah, Duhok, functions well. Civil administration works, and the British Consulate in Erbil gives direct, resident consular support. Cases here tend to move at the faster end of what’s realistic.

item.title: Repatriation from Kyrgyzstan: The Translator Bottleneck
item.description:
Kyrgyzstan isn’t difficult because anyone is working against a family here. The British Embassy in Bishkek is established and experienced. What makes this one of the more demanding Central Asian corridors is a combination of geography, thin translator supply, and mountains that draw exactly the kind of British visitor who ends up needing this service.

Why the translator issue comes first

Deal with this before anything else. Kyrgyz uses Cyrillic script but is its own distinct language, not a dialect of Russian, Kazakh, or Uzbek, and it needs a translator specifically accredited in Kyrgyz. Very few exist in the UK. Contact the British Embassy in Bishkek the moment you’re notified of the death and ask specifically for their current recommended translators, before documents even arrive. A translator lined up in advance can turn work around quickly. Starting that search once paperwork is already in hand adds days you don’t need to lose.

item.title: Repatriation from Liberia: What the English Documentation Means
item.description:
Founded in the 1820s by freed American and Caribbean settlers, Liberia kept the legal and administrative language of its founders. That single historical fact makes a real practical difference today: English runs through every stage of a Liberian repatriation case, and there’s a resident British Embassy in Monrovia to go with it.

What that actually means for a family

No certified translation step. That alone sets Liberia apart from most of its West African neighbours, where French or Portuguese documentation adds real time and cost before a case can even reach the UK end of the process. The British Embassy in Monrovia registers the death and liaises directly with Liberian authorities on the family’s behalf.

item.title: What Happens When Someone Dies in Monaco
item.description:
Monaco is genuinely one of the more straightforward places for this to happen, which isn’t something that can be said about most of Europe. A well-functioning administration, proximity to a major airport, and a legal system built on French civil law all help.

The paperwork runs through Monaco’s own registry

The Direction de l’Etat Civil issues the death certificate. It’s in French, and it needs certified English translation before UK authorities will accept it. If the death wasn’t natural, the Sûreté Publique, Monaco’s police force, investigates first and has to authorise release of the body before anything else can move forward.

item.title: Bringing Someone Home from Guinea-Bissau
item.description:
Guinea-Bissau’s repatriation process runs on Portuguese civil law, a legacy of colonial administration, and on a UK diplomatic arrangement that puts the nearest support two countries away.

Support from Dakar, not Bissau

No UK diplomat is resident in Bissau. The non-resident British Embassy in Dakar, Senegal, handles consular matters, on +221 33 823 7392, and FCDO advises contacting them directly for any assistance while in Guinea-Bissau. The FCDO Emergency Travel Line, 020 7008 5000, is available at any hour from anywhere.

item.title: Bringing Someone Home from The Gambia
item.description:
The Gambia is the smallest country on mainland Africa, and for winter sun visitors from the UK, one of the busiest. That volume means the system for handling a British death here is genuinely practised, not improvised.

Why this corridor is more manageable

English is the official language. The Gambia is a Commonwealth member. The British High Commission in Banjul is resident, not covering the country from a neighbouring capital the way so many West African corridors work. Every document, from the death certificate through to the export permit, is produced in English. None of that removes the grief. It does remove a layer of friction most families in this position have to deal with elsewhere.

item.title: Finland to UK Repatriation: A Family Guide
item.description:
Finland ranks among the least complicated European repatriations for a UK family, and the Strasbourg Convention is the main reason why. Both countries are signatories, so the transport permit issued by Finnish authorities carries straight through UK entry formalities. There’s no extra legalisation step to chase.

The Strasbourg Convention advantage

Valvira, Finland’s National Supervisory Authority for Welfare and Health, issues the Matkustuslupa, the international transport permit. Because the UK and Finland both signed the Strasbourg Convention on the Transfer of Mortal Remains, that permit is accepted directly by UK authorities. Countries outside the Convention need an additional consular legalisation stage. Finland doesn’t.

item.title: Grenada to UK Repatriation: What Families Ask
item.description:
Grenada is known to most British visitors for its beaches and its diving, and to a smaller but significant group as the home of St George’s University, one of the Caribbean’s largest medical schools. Both groups die here occasionally, and the process for bringing them home is the same one.

No resident post, but a working system

There’s no resident British diplomatic post in Grenada. An Honorary British Consul in St George’s is the first point of contact, backed by the British High Commission in Bridgetown, Barbados, which holds formal accreditation for the island. Call the Honorary Consul first, then the FCDO Emergency Travel Line, 020 7008 5000, if you’re not already through to local support.

item.title: Guyana to UK Repatriation: Logistics Explained
item.description:
Guyana looks straightforward on paper. English is the official language, the legal system follows English common law, and the British High Commission in Georgetown is resident. Then you look at the map, and two-thirds of the country is jungle.

What makes Guyana manageable

A resident British High Commission in Georgetown, English-language documentation throughout, and English common law procedures put Guyana well ahead of most South American repatriation destinations for a UK family. The General Register Office issues the death certificate once the attending physician has certified cause of death. For non-natural deaths, a post-mortem at Georgetown Public Hospital, and sometimes a Magistrate’s inquest, comes first.

item.title: Repatriation from Gabon: The Legal Process
item.description:
Gabon’s repatriation process runs on French civil law, and since August 2023 it has also run under a military transition government. Neither fact should alarm a family working with the right specialist, but both shape what happens next.

Where consular support comes from

There’s no British High Commission in Gabon itself. Coverage is non-resident, from the British High Commission in Yaounde, Cameroon, on +237 222 220 796. For urgent help at any hour, call the FCDO Emergency Travel Line on 020 7008 5000.

item.title: Repatriation from Georgia: What to Expect
item.description:
Georgia isn’t a Strasbourg Convention signatory, so the full consular pathway applies, and one detail catches almost every family out: the paperwork isn’t just in a foreign language, it’s in a script most translators have never seen.

The Mkhedruli script problem

Georgian uses Mkhedruli, a script unique to the country and unrelated to Cyrillic or Latin alphabets. Most translation agencies simply don’t carry an accredited Georgian legal translator. What you need is specific: someone accredited for Georgian legal and administrative language, recognised by the British Embassy Tbilisi, and familiar with how the Georgian Civil Registry Agency formats its documents. Ask the Embassy for its current approved list immediately. Waiting until documents are issued to start that search adds three to five days you don’t need to lose.

item.title: Repatriation from Guatemala Explained
item.description:
Guatemala draws British visitors to its colonial architecture, its volcanoes, and its Mayan ruins, and each of those draws carries a different repatriation profile depending on exactly where the death happens.

The forensic gatekeeper: INACIF

Every death classified as unnatural in Guatemala goes through the Instituto Nacional de Ciencias Forenses, the national forensic institute based in Guatemala City. Trauma, accidents, or any unclear cause of death trigger a formal INACIF autopsy, and the body can’t be released until the Ministerio Publico, the prosecutor’s office, signs off. A death outside the capital has to transfer there first. That transfer, not the INACIF process itself, is usually what adds the most time.

item.title: Repatriation from Guinea: A Family Guide
item.description:
Guinea sits on West Africa’s Atlantic coast, bordered by six countries, and has been under military rule since September 2021. For a UK family, three facts shape the whole process: no resident British Embassy, French civil law throughout, and Air France still flying to Conakry despite the political change.

Consular coverage from Freetown

There’s no British Embassy in Guinea. All consular support for British nationals comes from the British High Commission in Freetown, Sierra Leone, on +232 22 232 961, which registers the death for UK purposes and advises on local documentation channels. Call the FCDO Emergency Travel Line first, 020 7008 5000, available around the clock. FCDO currently advises against all but essential travel to Guinea.

item.title: Repatriation from Haiti: What Families Need to Know
item.description:
Haiti is, by most measures, the hardest repatriation corridor a UK family can face. That’s not an exaggeration for effect. It’s the honest starting point for anyone dealing with a death here right now.

The security situation, plainly stated

Since President Jovenel Moise’s assassination in July 2021, criminal gangs, particularly the G9 alliance and the Viv Ansanm coalition, have taken control of large parts of Port-au-Prince and the surrounding Artibonite department. A Kenya-led Multinational Security Support Mission, authorised under UN Security Council Resolution 2699, began deploying in 2024. As of mid-2026, security in much of the capital and Artibonite remains severe. In practice, this can mean the body and the civil registration office you need are both in an area no one can safely reach for days or weeks at a time.

item.title: Bosnia and Herzegovina: Which Entity Matters
item.description:
Bosnia and Herzegovina looks like a single country on a map. Administratively, it isn’t. Two parallel systems handle death registration, forensic examination, and export permits, and knowing which one applies to your case matters from the first phone call.

Two entities, two systems

The Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina covers Sarajevo, Mostar, Travnik, and Tuzla. Republika Srpska covers Banja Luka, Trebinje, and East Sarajevo. Each registers deaths through its own municipal civil records office, known as the Matičar, and each has a separate forensic institute for non-natural deaths. A case in Sarajevo and a case in Banja Luka follow entirely different administrative paths, even though both end at the same British Embassy.

item.title: Bringing Someone Home from Botswana
item.description:
The Okavango Delta draws British visitors precisely because it’s remote: no roads, no towns, just water channels and bush camps reached by small aircraft. That same remoteness is the first thing a family needs to understand if a death happens there.

Getting out of the Delta comes first

Bush camps, private concessions, and the Moremi Game Reserve have no road access whatsoever. A death anywhere in the Delta requires charter aircraft or helicopter evacuation to Maun Airport before documentation can start. Depending on the camp’s location and aircraft availability, that evacuation alone takes 1 to 4 hours. Add ground or further air transfer to Gaborone for anything beyond a straightforward case, and the pre-documentation delay for a remote Delta death can run 24 to 96 hours before the formal process even opens.

item.title: Bringing Someone Home from Cabo Verde
item.description:
Cabo Verde has become one of the most popular UK package holiday destinations in West Africa, with direct flights from regional airports straight to Sal and Boa Vista. Most cases start at a resort on one of those two islands. Some don’t, and that’s where the timeline changes.

Ten islands, one repatriation route

The archipelago spans roughly 400 kilometres of open Atlantic. Sal and Boa Vista, where most British visitors stay, connect fairly directly to international cargo routing. Fogo, Santo Antao, Sao Nicolau, Sao Vicente, Brava, and Maio don’t have that connection. A death on any of these smaller islands means the remains travel to Sal or Santiago first, by air on Cabo Verde Airlines or charter, or in some cases by a ferry-and-air combination. That inter-island leg alone typically adds 3 to 7 days before the main international flight is even booked.

item.title: Bringing Someone Home from Chile
item.description:
Chile isn’t a difficult country to repatriate from. It’s a country where the paperwork is straightforward and the geography does most of the work of making things hard.

Where the forensic service can and can’t reach

The Servicio Medico Legal is Chile’s national forensic medicine service, and it’s genuinely well established, with offices in Santiago, Valparaiso, Concepcion, La Serena, Antofagasta, and other regional capitals. What it doesn’t have is any presence in Patagonia. A death at Torres del Paine National Park, which draws thousands of British trekkers every year, has to be moved to Punta Arenas for a first examination. From there it may need to go on to Santiago for a complete analysis. Neither step is optional, and neither is quick.

item.title: Bringing Someone Home from Djibouti
item.description:
Djibouti sits at the southern gateway to the Red Sea, small in size but strategically significant enough to host both French and American military bases. For UK repatriation purposes, it’s a workable process shaped by two facts that surprise most families: no resident British Embassy, and every document produced in French and Arabic.

Consular support, routed through Ethiopia

There is no British Embassy in Djibouti itself. All consular matters, including death registration for UK purposes, are handled remotely by the British Embassy in Addis Ababa, which holds non-resident accreditation for the country. Call the FCDO Emergency Travel Line first, on 020 7008 5000, available at any hour, or the Addis Ababa Embassy directly on +251 11 617 0100. Managing a case with a specialist firm that has French-speaking East African contacts makes a real difference here, since coordination happens across a border rather than within one office.

item.title: Bringing Someone Home from the Dominican Republic
item.description:
Most British visitors to the Dominican Republic never leave the Punta Cana resort corridor, and that single fact shapes almost everything about how a repatriation from here plays out. From Cap Cana down to Uvero Alto, this stretch of coast sees the overwhelming majority of British deaths in the country too.

That concentration cuts both ways. Local funeral directors here are genuinely experienced with foreign national cases, thanks to the sheer volume of American and European tourism. But two structural facts still shape every case: there’s no resident British Embassy, and INACIF, the national forensic institute, gets involved more often than families expect.

item.title: Brunei Repatriation: What Families Ask First
item.description:
Brunei is one of the more straightforward Southeast Asian repatriation cases, with a resident British High Commission and a direct flight home. The one question that shapes everything else is religious classification.

Why classification is the first question

Islam is Brunei’s state religion, and the Malay Islamic Monarchy framework runs through much of public life, including how deaths are processed. Non-Muslim deaths go through the civil registry only, with a straightforward path to a death certificate in Malay and English. Muslim deaths also require clearance from the Department of Religious Affairs (Jabatan Hal Ehwal Ugama Islam) before any export permit can be issued. Neither path is dramatically slower than the other, but they’re genuinely different processes, and knowing which applies from the outset avoids wasted steps.

item.title: Burundi Repatriation: The Kampala Connection
item.description:
Burundi is a small, landlocked country bordering Rwanda, Tanzania, and the Democratic Republic of Congo, and its repatriation process is shaped as much by where the nearest British diplomatic post sits as by anything happening inside the country itself.

Consular support comes from across a border

The British High Commission in Kampala, Uganda, handles every consular matter connected to Burundi. There’s no resident post in Bujumbura. That non-resident arrangement means the usual back-and-forth of registering a death and coordinating with local authorities happens with an extra layer of distance built in. A specialist repatriation firm with established contacts inside Burundi can close that gap far more effectively than a family trying to manage it directly from the UK.

item.title: Cambodia Repatriation: Questions Families Ask
item.description:
Cambodia’s tourist infrastructure has grown fast around Siem Reap and Phnom Penh, but the systems that handle a death haven’t kept pace in the same way. Knowing the shape of the process before you need it makes a genuine difference.

The first calls to make

Local police answer on 117, fire on 118, and ambulance on 119. Cambodian hospital capacity is inconsistent: expect a reasonable standard in Phnom Penh’s better facilities, and considerably less in rural areas or smaller towns. Contact the British Embassy in Phnom Penh as soon as the immediate emergency response is underway, since its consular capacity for remote cases in Siem Reap or Sihanoukville is limited and earlier contact helps.

item.title: Central African Republic: What Families Need to Know
item.description:
The Central African Republic is consistently ranked among the world’s most fragile states, and that reality shapes every part of a repatriation case from the very first phone call.

Consular coverage from another country

CAR has no resident British Embassy. Every consular matter for British nationals goes through the British Embassy in Yaounde, Cameroon, which registers the death and advises on what’s realistically achievable given current conditions on the ground. This non-resident arrangement means a case starts with an extra step of coordination that most corridors don’t require.

item.title: Chad Repatriation: What the Transition Means
item.description:
N’Djamena runs on a functioning civil process. Most of the rest of the country doesn’t, and that gap is the first thing a family needs to understand about repatriation from Chad.

A government still finding its footing

Chad has been under a Transitional Military Council since President Idriss Deby was killed in April 2021. His son, Mahamat Idriss Deby, has led it since. A new constitution was approved by referendum in 2024, and elections are planned, but the administrative machinery hasn’t fully settled into a permanent shape yet. Practically, that means routine steps, such as issuing a death certificate through the etat civil, can take longer under a transitional authority than they would under an established government.

item.title: Colombia: The Permit Families Don't Expect
item.description:
Cartagena’s walled city, the coffee region around Salento, the Lost City trek: Colombia has become one of the fastest-growing destinations for British travellers in the past decade. The documentation process that follows a death there, though, is unlike almost anything else in South America.

A permit families rarely see coming

Most countries route the export permit for human remains through a health ministry. Colombia doesn’t. It goes through INVIMA, the Instituto Nacional de Vigilancia de Medicamentos y Alimentos, an agency families and even some funeral directors associate with food and medicine regulation rather than repatriation. It’s a genuine step, not a technicality, and it typically takes 3 to 7 days once everything else, including the death registration and any forensic clearance, is already in hand.

item.title: Comoros: Which Island Changes Everything
item.description:
Ask where in Comoros the death happened before anything else. It’s the single question that decides how the rest of a case unfolds.

Three islands, one gateway

Grande Comore, Anjouan, and Moheli each run their own local administration under the federal government of the Union of the Comoros, but only Grande Comore, home to the capital Moroni, has an airport capable of handling international cargo. A death on Anjouan or Moheli means an inter-island transfer first, typically by small charter aircraft or boat, before the wider process can even begin. It’s rarely more than a day or two of delay, but it’s a step families aren’t always told to expect.

item.title: Congo-Brazzaville: Not the Country Next Door
item.description:
Two countries share the name Congo, sit on opposite banks of the same river, and are frequently mixed up by people who’ve never needed to tell them apart before. For a family arranging repatriation, getting this right from the first phone call matters.

Not the country you might be picturing

The Republic of Congo, often written as Congo-Brazzaville to keep it distinct, is a separate nation from the Democratic Republic of Congo, whose capital is Kinshasa. They border each other along the Congo River, and their governments, legal systems, and repatriation procedures have nothing in common beyond the name. If a specialist starts talking about Kinshasa when a death happened in Brazzaville, that’s actually correct: UK consular coverage for the Republic of Congo comes from the British Embassy in Kinshasa, reachable on +243 81 556 0200, as a non-resident function. The FCDO Emergency Travel Line, 020 7008 5000, should be the first call in any case.

item.title: Cuba: The Autopsy You Can't Decline
item.description:
Cuba isn’t a country where a family gets to make many choices about how a repatriation unfolds. Almost every step is fixed by state process, and understanding that from the outset is the most useful thing anyone can do at the start.

An autopsy with no exceptions

Medicina Legal, Cuba’s state forensic authority, examines every foreign national who dies in the country. Not just the unexplained or violent cases: every one. There’s no route to declining it, and results typically take 7 to 14 days. Nothing else in the process can move until that examination is complete and signed off, which makes it the single biggest driver of how long a Cuba case takes overall.

item.title: Denmark Repatriation: Fast, Unless It's Greenland
item.description:
Denmark is, for most cases, about as straightforward as European repatriation gets. It’s also a country whose territory stretches into the Arctic, and that second fact changes everything for a smaller number of families.

Why mainland cases move quickly

Both Denmark and the UK sit under the Strasbourg Convention on the Transfer of Mortal Remains, a 1973 agreement that means Denmark’s transport permit, the Udrejsetilladelse, is accepted by UK authorities as it stands, without the extra consular legalisation steps a non-signatory country would require. Add direct flights from Copenhagen Kastrup with SAS and British Airways, and an efficient forensic system through the Retsmedicinsk Institut, and it’s easy to see why most mainland cases run just 7 to 12 days.

item.title: DR Congo: Kinshasa and Everywhere Else
item.description:
Ask where in DR Congo the death happened, and the answer shapes everything that follows: whether the case is difficult, very difficult, or, in the worst circumstances, not currently achievable at all.

Kinshasa: the workable half of the country

Unlike much of Central Africa, DR Congo has a resident British Embassy, in Kinshasa, fully operational and reachable on +243 81 555 8900. It registers the death and issues the documentation a family needs directly. The FCDO Emergency Travel Line, 020 7008 5000, is still the right first call at any hour.

item.title: Repatriation from Bolivia: The Altitude Question
item.description:
A holiday to Lake Titicaca or the Uyuni Salt Flat can turn into something far more serious within hours. Altitude sickness kills UK visitors to Bolivia every year, and the legal process that follows catches most families by surprise.

Why altitude deaths aren’t treated as natural

Soroche can cause fatal pulmonary or cerebral oedema with almost no warning, sometimes within a day of arriving at altitude. Bolivian law still classifies it as an unnatural death. That means the FELCC and the Fiscalia take jurisdiction before anything else can happen, regardless of how clear the medical cause appears to the family or the attending doctor. Do not assume this step can be skipped because the cause seems obvious.

item.title: Repatriation from Burkina Faso: What to Expect
item.description:
Burkina Faso sits among the more demanding repatriation cases in West Africa, not because the underlying documentation process is unusual, but because of what surrounds it: no resident Embassy, a junta government, and active armed group presence across large parts of the country.

No Embassy on the ground

There has been no resident British Embassy in Burkina Faso since before the current government took power. The British High Commission in Accra, Ghana, handles every consular matter for British nationals in Burkina Faso, coordinating death registration and family liaison from outside the country rather than through a local post. This remote coordination is standard practice for this corridor, not a temporary gap.

item.title: Repatriation from Cameroon: The Language Question
item.description:
Cameroon carries a colonial-era administrative split that still shapes how a death is processed today. Knowing which system applies, French or English, is the first question any family needs answered.

A country administered two ways

After the First World War, Cameroon was divided between French and British mandate rule. The division never fully disappeared. The eight Francophone regions, including the capital Yaounde and the commercial centre Douala, follow French civil law tradition, with documentation in French. The North West and South West regions retained English-language administration from the British mandate period. Which system applies to a given case depends entirely on where in the country the death occurred, and it determines both the language of every document and the administrative process itself.

item.title: Repatriation from Costa Rica: What to Expect
item.description:
Costa Rica draws more British visitors than any other Central American country, and most of that traffic goes exactly where you’d expect: national parks, rainforest, volcanoes, surf breaks. It’s also where the OIJ, the country’s judicial investigation police, takes an unusually active role, even in cases that look entirely accidental.

Routine, not suspicion

Deaths at Corcovado, Arenal volcano, or Monteverde get an OIJ investigation as standard, whatever the apparent cause. That surprises some families, who assume police involvement signals something wrong. In Costa Rica it’s simply how the system works for these locations, and it means the body stays where it is until the OIJ has finished its on-site assessment. Post-mortems, where they’re needed, happen at INCIENSA or OIJ forensic facilities in San Jose, and results usually take 7 to 14 days.

item.title: Repatriation from Dominica: A Family Guide
item.description:
Dominica calls itself the Nature Isle, and it earns the name. Rainforest, hot springs, a volcanic spine running the length of the island: it’s a place people come to for the landscape itself, not resort beaches. That same landscape is exactly what makes a repatriation from here different from most of its Caribbean neighbours.

Do not confuse it with the Dominican Republic. They are separate countries, separate systems, separate everything except a similar name.

item.title: Repatriation from Ecuador: What Families Ask
item.description:
Ecuador draws British visitors for two very different reasons: the Andean highlands around Quito, and the Galapagos, one of the most tightly protected archipelagos on earth. Those two draws create two very different repatriation cases, and the difference matters enormously for how long a family should expect to wait.

The Fiscalia’s role

For any death that isn’t straightforwardly natural, jurisdiction passes to the Fiscalia General del Estado, Ecuador’s prosecutor’s office. It orders a forensic post-mortem, and the body cannot be released until that process concludes and the Fiscalia issues its clearance. This is, without question, the single document that determines the pace of an Ecuadorian case.

item.title: Repatriation from El Salvador: Logistics Explained
item.description:
El Salvador has changed enormously over the past few years. Government security measures introduced from 2022 transformed a homicide rate that once made headlines, and tourism, surfing on the Pacific coast, coffee country, colonial Suchitoto, has grown steadily since. British deaths still happen here, though, and the repatriation process is more layered than the country’s compact size might suggest.

Two offices, one authority

There’s no resident British Embassy in El Salvador. What exists instead is an Honorary Consul in San Salvador, who handles the immediate practical steps: registering the death, liaising with the family, witnessing documents. Real authority, the formal registration and repatriation sign-off that actually matters, rests with the British Embassy in Guatemala City, 350 kilometres away.

item.title: Repatriation from Equatorial Guinea Explained
item.description:
Equatorial Guinea is the only Spanish-speaking country in sub-Saharan Africa, a fact that surprises most people the first time they hear it. Geography adds a second layer of complexity: the country is split between Bioko Island, where the capital Malabo sits, and Rio Muni, a separate mainland territory wedged between Cameroon and Gabon. Every international repatriation exits through Malabo, whatever else is true about a case.

No resident Embassy

The United Kingdom has no Embassy in Equatorial Guinea. Consular cover comes from the British High Commission in Yaounde, Cameroon, handling the country remotely. The FCDO Emergency Travel Line, 020 7008 5000, is the right first call at any hour; the High Commission itself can be reached on +237 222 220 796.

item.title: Repatriation from Eritrea: What to Expect
item.description:
Most families who contact us about Eritrea are part of the UK’s substantial Eritrean diaspora, largely based in London, either visiting relatives or supporting relatives who live there permanently. It’s worth saying plainly at the outset: Eritrea is one of the most closed administrative environments in the world, and that single fact governs everything else about how a case unfolds.

A government that controls every step

Eritrea has no independent judiciary and no free press. The People’s Front for Democracy and Justice, the ruling party, oversees essentially every administrative decision, including how a case like this proceeds. The British Embassy in Asmara is a resident mission, which helps, but its capacity to influence a domestic Eritrean timeline is limited in ways that surprise families used to more responsive systems elsewhere.

item.title: Repatriation from Estonia: The Process Explained
item.description:
Estonia rarely produces the kind of case that keeps a repatriation coordinator awake at night, and that’s worth saying up front, because most of the countries we write about here don’t offer that reassurance. As an EU, NATO, and Schengen member with one of the world’s most advanced digital public administration systems, Estonia’s civil processes work the way they’re supposed to.

The Population Register does the heavy lifting

Death registration runs through the Rahvastikuregister, Estonia’s Population Register, administered by the Ministry of Interior. It’s part of the same e-governance infrastructure Estonia is internationally known for, and it means death certificates are issued efficiently, without the layers of separate sign-offs that slow things down elsewhere. For a straightforward natural death, this alone can keep a case moving in days rather than weeks.

item.title: Repatriation from Eswatini: A Family Guide
item.description:
Eswatini, renamed from Swaziland in 2018, is a country many families haven’t heard much about before a death brings it into their lives. It’s small, landlocked, tucked between South Africa and Mozambique, and for repatriation purposes it’s genuinely one of the more manageable Southern African cases. English is an official language throughout. That single fact removes a whole layer of difficulty that families face elsewhere in the region.

Who typically dies here

Most British deaths in Eswatini involve tourists at Hlane Royal National Park or Mkhaya Game Reserve, business travellers, NGO and development sector staff, and members of the small but established UK-Swazi diaspora community. It’s not a high-volume corridor, but the British High Commission in Mbabane, a resident mission, is familiar with the process.

item.title: Repatriation from Ethiopia: Logistics Explained
item.description:
Ethiopia sits high on the list of places British travellers go for genuine adventure: the rock-hewn churches of Lalibela, the Simien Mountains, the Omo Valley’s tribal regions, the Blue Nile falls. It’s also a country where the honest answer to “how long will this take” depends enormously on exactly where the death occurred.

Addis Ababa is where everything has to happen

Ethiopia isn’t a signatory to the Strasbourg Agreement, so repatriation runs through domestic Ethiopian law and bilateral consular arrangements rather than a standardised international framework. A forensic post-mortem, where one is needed, can only take place in Addis Ababa, typically at St. Paul’s Hospital Millennium Medical College. That single constraint drives almost everything else about timing.

item.title: Repatriation from Fiji: What Families Ask
item.description:
Fiji is, by Pacific standards, one of the more manageable repatriations, and it’s worth saying that clearly, because most of the region’s smaller nations present far greater difficulty. English is the language of official documentation throughout, which removes the translation delay that complicates so many other corridors. The British High Commission in Suva is experienced with British national cases. The real variable here isn’t the paperwork. It’s geography.

Viti Levu against everywhere else

Viti Levu is the main island, home to both Nadi International Airport and the capital Suva. A death anywhere on Viti Levu, including the Coral Coast, moves through the standard documentation chain cleanly. Vanua Levu, the second largest island, needs a domestic flight transfer to Viti Levu first, typically one to two days.

item.title: Argentina Repatriation: What Families Ask First
item.description:
Argentina draws more British visitors than almost anywhere else in South America, and Buenos Aires, Patagonia, and the Mendoza wine region each bring a different kind of repatriation case. The single biggest variable isn’t geography. It’s whether a judge needs to get involved.

The judicial release requirement

For a natural death with a clear medical cause, Argentina’s process is manageable: registration at the Registro Civil, an Acta de Defuncion, and a route to the UK via Madrid or Sao Paulo since no direct flights exist. Add unnatural, sudden, or suspicious circumstances, though, and the Poder Judicial takes over. A judge must order the release of the body before an undertaker can even begin embalming. Buenos Aires courts tend to move at a reasonable pace. Provincial courts, especially in Patagonia, do not always share that pace.

item.title: Bahamas Repatriation: Questions Families Ask
item.description:
The Bahamas is a Commonwealth country with English as its official language, a British High Commission in Nassau, and consular procedures that are, by regional standards, well established. That’s the reassuring part. The complicated part is that it’s also around 700 islands.

Nassau and Freeport: two separate systems

For a death on New Providence, where Nassau sits, or on Grand Bahama, home to Freeport, the process is relatively clear, but it isn’t the same process. Nassau cases go through the Department of Pathology at Princess Margaret Hospital. Freeport and Grand Bahama cases go through Rand Memorial Hospital’s own pathology department instead. It’s worth knowing which island applies before picking up the phone, because contacts for one don’t transfer to the other.

item.title: Belarus Repatriation: What Families Need to Know
item.description:
Belarus is one of the harder cases on this site, and it’s worth being direct about that from the outset rather than glossing over it. The British national population there is small: business visitors mostly, some family visitors connected to the Belarusian diaspora, a limited number of aid workers. Cases are rare. When they happen, several things complicate them at once.

Reduced embassy capacity is the starting condition

The British Embassy in Minsk remains open, but with significantly reduced staff following the diplomatic deterioration since 2020. It still registers deaths of British nationals and issues the notifications needed for UK purposes, but families should expect this to take longer than an equivalent case in a country with a fully staffed post. The FCDO Emergency Travel Line, +44 (0)20 7008 5000, operates 24 hours; the British Embassy Minsk can be reached on +375 17 229 82 00.

item.title: Benin Repatriation: What Families Ask First
item.description:
A death in Benin brings together three things that don’t often appear together: French civil law documentation, non-resident UK consular support based in a different country, and, in the north, a genuine security situation. None of this needs to feel overwhelming if it’s understood from the start.

Accra, not Cotonou

There’s no British diplomat living in Benin. The non-resident British High Commission in Accra, Ghana, covers Benin’s consular needs, and it can be reached on +233 302 213 250. Start with the FCDO’s 24-hour line, +44 (0)20 7008 5000, and let them coordinate with Accra on the specifics; it’s a more direct route than trying to work out the Accra contact details alone in the middle of a difficult day.

item.title: Bringing Someone Home from Azerbaijan
item.description:
Baku’s restored old town and its position at the meeting point of the South Caucasus and the Caspian Sea have made it a genuine draw for British business travellers and tourists. On the logistics side, Azerbaijan has one advantage its neighbours don’t.

The direct flight that actually matters

Azerbaijan Airlines flies Baku Heydar Aliyev International Airport to London Heathrow directly, and it carries cargo. Nowhere else in the Caucasus or Central Asia offers that combination; everywhere else routes through Istanbul or Dubai first. This single fact is why Azerbaijan sits toward the more manageable end of repatriations from the wider region, even though the underlying documentation requirements are just as thorough as anywhere else.

item.title: Bringing Someone Home from Belize
item.description:
Belize stands apart from its Central American neighbours in a way that matters directly for repatriation: English is the official language, and it’s the region’s only Commonwealth member. Both facts show up immediately in how a case unfolds. What doesn’t show up immediately is how much the geography still complicates things.

Belmopan, not Belize City

The British High Commission sits in Belmopan, the capital, roughly an hour and a half west of Belize City on the Western Highway. Belize City remains the commercial and population centre, and for urgent situations there, the High Commission can respond without much difficulty. They’re the first official contact for a British national death anywhere in the country, and their working relationship with Karl Heusner Memorial Hospital in Belize City is well established.

item.title: Death at a Sports Event Abroad: What Happens
item.description:
Marathons, club rugby tours, sailing regattas, five-a-side tournaments played on a works trip: British participation in organised sport abroad is large, and deaths during these events are not as rare as families assume until it happens to someone they know. The complication that catches people out first is rarely the process. It’s the insurance.

The gap most families don’t check for

Standard travel insurance was built around holidays, not competition. MoneyHelper’s guidance is clear that adventure sports, winter sports, and other activities insurers treat as high risk are commonly excluded from ordinary policies. Competing at national level, or on a professional or semi-professional basis, can exclude cover entirely under some policies, regardless of the sport itself.

item.title: Death in a Care Facility Abroad: Repatriation
item.description:
Many British retirees live out their later years in care homes and nursing facilities abroad, most commonly in Spain, Portugal, France, and Cyprus. When death comes in one of these settings, it’s rarely a shock to the facility, even if it still is to the family. That familiarity changes the early stages of the process, though not the ones that follow.

Why certification is often quicker here

A long-term resident’s medical history is already on file. The attending doctor, sometimes visiting regularly for years, has documented the relevant conditions. This puts a care facility death closer to a hospital death than to an unattended one: the doctor can usually certify cause without waiting on a separate confirmation step, because there’s nothing unexplained to confirm.

item.title: Death in a Hotel Room Abroad: What Happens
item.description:
Someone dies alone in a hotel room. No doctor was present. No one else was in the room. Whatever the actual cause turns out to be, most countries will not simply take a family’s word for it, and neither will most hotels move fast to explain what happens next.

Why this isn’t treated like an ordinary death

A death with no witnesses and no medical attendance sits differently in almost every country’s system than one that happened in a hospital bed under a doctor’s care. Local police attend. A doctor, sometimes a forensic examiner, confirms there’s nothing else at play before any certificate is issued. It’s worth saying plainly: this is routine, not suspicion of wrongdoing. Authorities can’t certify a cause they haven’t confirmed, and an empty room gives them nothing to go on except the examination itself.

item.title: Death in Custody Abroad: UK Family Rights
item.description:
A death in custody, whether in a police cell or a prison, abroad raises questions that go well beyond repatriation logistics. Families want to know who investigates, whether anyone independent is watching, and what rights they actually have. The honest answer has more edges to it than families are usually told.

What the FCDO can and cannot do

GOV.UK’s guidance for British nationals detained abroad is direct on this point: investigating the circumstances of a death is the responsibility of the local authorities, not the FCDO. The Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office cannot interfere in another country’s investigative or judicial process. Diplomatic channels can be used to press for information, to ask questions the family wants answered, and to request an independent post-mortem where one is wanted. None of that amounts to the UK running or overseeing the investigation.

item.title: Death in Hospital Abroad: The Release Procedure
item.description:
A hospital is, in one respect, the easiest place for a death abroad to happen. The doctor already knows the patient. The cause of death is documented. There’s no police report to wait for, no forensic examiner to book in.

That’s the good news. The complication is different, and it catches families off guard: the hospital’s own billing department.

Why the medical side moves quickly

When someone dies under a doctor’s care, with a known diagnosis and a visible treatment history, most countries’ medical certification is straightforward. The attending physician signs the cause of death. There’s rarely a need for the police or forensic involvement that an unattended death elsewhere would trigger. Compare this to a sudden collapse in a hotel room with no witnesses, where authorities typically want confirmation before they’ll certify anything.

item.title: Repatriation from Afghanistan: Questions Families Ask
item.description:
Afghanistan is the most difficult repatriation case a UK family can face, and it needs saying plainly rather than softened. The FCDO advises against all travel to Afghanistan, the UK Embassy in Kabul has been closed since August 2021, and the country the UK deals with on paper is not one it formally recognises as a government.

No resident embassy to call

Call the FCDO first, on +44 (0)20 7008 5000, available 24 hours. This is the essential first step, not an optional one. The British Embassy in Islamabad, Pakistan offers what consular assistance is possible for Afghanistan-connected cases, but there is no equivalent post inside the country itself.

item.title: Repatriation from Algeria: Questions Families Ask
item.description:
Algeria has one of the largest Algerian-heritage communities in Western Europe, and many British nationals of Algerian descent travel regularly to visit family there. That makes Algeria a more frequent repatriation origin than its tourism numbers alone would suggest, and families dealing with it for the first time tend to ask the same handful of questions.

Why the province matters more than the country

Algeria has no single national authority issuing body transport permits. Each of its 58 wilayas handles this itself through the laissez-passer mortuaire. Algiers wilaya has seen enough international cases to move at a reasonable pace. Tamanrasset, Illizi, and Adrar, in the remote south, have not, and processing there can be markedly slower. Where a death occurs outside Algiers, moving the body there before starting the permit process is often the faster path overall, even accounting for the transfer itself.

item.title: Repatriation from Andorra: Questions Families Ask
item.description:
Andorra sits in the Pyrenees between France and Spain, small enough to have no airport and no armed forces of its own, yet with a distinct legal and civil system built on Catalan administration. British visitors come mainly for skiing, hiking, and duty-free shopping, and questions about repatriation from Andorra tend to centre on one fact: there’s no airport to fly from.

The road transfer is the defining logistics step

Every repatriation from Andorra starts the same way: a road transfer out of the country, because there is nowhere to fly from directly. Barcelona El Prat, roughly three hours south by road, offers multiple direct daily flights to the UK and is the choice for most cases. Toulouse-Blagnac, a similar distance via the northern route, has fewer direct UK connections but remains a viable alternative depending on where the case originates and which route suits the specific logistics.

item.title: Repatriation from Angola: Questions Families Ask
item.description:
Bringing a loved one home from Angola is more involved than most families expect walking in, mainly because of two things: the paperwork is entirely in Portuguese, and funeral infrastructure outside Luanda is thin on the ground. If your relative worked in the oil sector, there’s a third factor that usually helps rather than hinders: an employer already used to this kind of situation.

What starts the process

For a sudden, unexplained, or violent death, the Servico de Investigacao Criminal, the SIC, must clear the body for release before any embalming or preparation can begin. A natural death in hospital moves faster: the attending doctor issues the Certidao de Obito, the civil registry records it, and the British Embassy in Luanda, which is resident and provides consular registration directly, can register the death without the additional SIC step.

item.title: Repatriation from Antigua and Barbuda: Family Questions
item.description:
Antigua is among the UK’s best-connected Caribbean destinations, and that connectivity shows up directly in how repatriation works. British Airways and Virgin Atlantic both fly direct from London Gatwick, which means cargo routing without the inter-island transit that most Eastern Caribbean repatriations require.

The flight connection that changes everything

Direct services to Gatwick mean a body can be consigned straight from V.C. Bird International Airport without first routing through a hub island, a step that adds real time and cost elsewhere in the Caribbean. Combined with English-language documentation under Antigua and Barbuda’s Commonwealth legal system, this is one of the more logistically straightforward Caribbean corridors on this site: 14 to 20 days for most cases, with the fastest straightforward cases on Antigua itself completing in 12 to 18 days.

item.title: Repatriation from Armenia: The Translation Delay
item.description:
Yerevan has quietly become a destination for cultural and short-break travel from the UK, and the British Embassy there is experienced with consular death cases. What catches most families off guard isn’t the process itself. It’s the script.

The script, not the paperwork, is the bottleneck

Armenian is written in its own alphabet, one with no relation to Cyrillic, Georgian, or Arabic. Every document that matters, the death certificate, the police record, the post-mortem report, the Ministry of Health export permit, is issued in it. Certified translation into English is required before UK purposes can be met, and the pool of UK-accredited Armenian legal translators is genuinely small. Contact the British Embassy Yerevan as soon as death is notified and ask for their current recommended translators before documents are even issued. Someone lined up in advance can start the moment paperwork is ready. Someone found afterward adds a week or more that didn’t need to happen.

item.title: Repatriation from Barbados: What to Expect
item.description:
A large resident British community and direct flights to London mean Barbados is one of the more manageable Caribbean corridors on this site. That reputation is earned, but it isn’t automatic. Barbadian law still has its own requirements, and being a Commonwealth country doesn’t skip any of them.

Registration comes first, and it forks early

A natural death with a clear cause goes through the Barbados Registration Department without much friction. Anything sudden, unexplained, or violent brings in the Royal Barbados Police Force, and the Coroner’s court may be convened from there. Where a post-mortem is ordered, it happens at the Queen Elizabeth Hospital in Bridgetown, and results are usually back within 5 to 10 days, a reasonable turnaround by regional standards.

item.title: Repatriation from Belgium: Family Questions
item.description:
Belgium sits near the easier end of European repatriations, and the reasons are structural rather than incidental. It’s an EU member state, a Strasbourg Convention signatory alongside the UK, and Brussels Airport has genuinely strong direct links to London. The one thing that catches families off guard is how local the administration actually is.

Everything runs through the commune

Unlike France or the UK, Belgium doesn’t centralise death registration nationally or even regionally. It happens at commune level. The local civil registry, the Service de l’Etat civil in Wallonia or the Dienst Burgerlijke Stand in Flanders, issues both the death certificate and the international transport permit that eventually lets remains leave the country. This has a knock-on effect on language: documents from a death in Flanders come out in Dutch, from Wallonia in French, and from the small German-speaking area around Liege and Eupen in German. All of it needs certified English translation regardless.

item.title: Repatriation from Bhutan: The Tour Operator Question
item.description:
Bhutan controls foreign tourism more tightly than almost anywhere else in the world, and that single fact shapes everything about how a death there is handled. Every visitor is on an approved tour with a licensed local guide, and that guide is often the first person who knows something has gone wrong.

The tour operator’s unusual role

Because every foreign visitor travels on a registered tour, the licensed Bhutanese operator has both a practical and, in effect, a coordinating role when a death occurs. They can liaise directly with local authorities and help open access to the documentation process faster than a family working from abroad could manage alone. Notify the operator immediately, alongside the FCDO on +44 (0)20 7008 5000.

item.title: Contacting the British Embassy After a Death Abroad
item.description:
Consular staff have done this before. That’s the main reason to call them, even though their role is narrower than most families expect.

Finding the right office

Don’t assume the nearest embassy is the right one. Some countries have a single embassy that covers consular matters for the whole territory; others split responsibilities across an embassy plus several honorary consulates. Gov.uk lists the correct post for the country where the death happened, along with an emergency out-of-hours number.

item.title: Drowning Abroad: Inquest and Repatriation
item.description:
Drowning is one of the more common causes of accidental death among British travellers, and one of the most consistently misunderstood in terms of what happens next.

Why investigation happens even when the cause looks obvious

A drowning at a beach, pool, or lake often looks unambiguous to the people who were there. Local authorities still need to formally establish it.
